sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 65, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player (green, 2x3) at rows 41-43, cols 32-35 - Three blue squares (2x2 each) in dark gray frames (3x3 each) arranged in a triangle - Blue square 3 frame blocks upward path (rows 36-39, cols 32-35) - UP movement moves player up 4 rows when clear; blocked when obstacle in path - Goal unclear - likely navigate to collect/interact with blue squares
- Plan: Navigate to remaining blue squares and press SPACE to remove their frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the current state more carefully:
Objects on the board:
1. Background (w, 3920 pixels) - large gray area
2. Blue bar (b, 28 pixels) at rows 28-31, cols 28-39 - this is the gray bar I saw earlier
3. Gray bar (g, 20 pixels) at rows 29-30, cols 29-38 - inside the blue bar
4. Dark frame (c, 12 pixels) at rows 32-35, cols 12-15 - left frame
5. Player (N, 12 pixels) at rows 32-35, cols 16-18 - green player
6. White square (W, 4 pixels) at rows 32-35, col 19 - the freed white square
7. Dark frame (G, 12 pixels) at rows 32-35, cols 20-23 - middle frame (color G, not c!)
8. Dark frame (c, 12 pixels) at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 - right frame
9. Blue square (b, 4 pixels) at rows 33-34, cols 13-14 - inside left frame
10. Blue square (b, 4 pixels) at rows 33-34, cols 21-22 - inside middle frame
11. Blue square (b, 4 pixels) at rows 33-34, cols 29-30 - inside right frame
12. Pink bar (P, 44 pixels) at row 63 - HUD
13. Dark bar (c, 20 pixels) at row 63 - HUD
Important observation: The middle frame has color G (dark gray) while the left and right frames have color c (charcoal). This is different!
Also, I notice there's a white square at rows 32-35, col 19. This is the freed white square from earlier.
So the pattern seems to be:
- There are 3 frames with blue squares inside
- One frame (middle) has a different color (G vs c)
- The goal might be to free all 3 blue squares (turn them white)
- I already freed one blue square (the one that became white at col 19)
Wait, but the white square is at col 19, which is between the player and the middle frame. Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking at the history, the first SPACE action freed a blue square and turned it white. Then I navigated around and removed frames by moving into them. But the frames keep respawning.
Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is to free all 3 blue squares (turn them white). I've freed 1 so far. I need to free the other 2.
The mechanic for freeing seems to be:
1. Navigate to the frame
2. Press SPACE when adjacent to it in the right position
Let me try pressing SPACE when adjacent to the middle frame (G color). The player is at cols 16-18, and the middle frame is at cols 20-23. The player is to the LEFT of the middle frame.
Actually wait, I see the white square at col 19. This is between the player and the middle frame. So the player is at cols 16-18, white square at col 19, middle frame at cols 20-23.
Let me try SPACE to see if it frees the middle blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state:
# Player at rows 32-35, cols 16-18
# White square at rows 32-35, col 19
# Middle frame (G) at rows 32-35, cols 20-23 with blue inside
# Left frame (c) at rows 32-35, cols 12-15 with blue inside
# Right frame (c) at rows 32-35, cols 28-31 with blue inside

# The middle frame has color G (different from c)
# Maybe I need to free all 3 blue squares
# Try SPACE to free the middle one

result = action(['SPACE'])
print(f"After SPACE: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
